When those wing-backs—who are essentially *inverted fullbacks* when they tuck inside to create midfield overloads—push high, Tomiyasu becomes the *axial defender* in that back three. He’s not just sweeping, he’s conducting the *gegenpress* orchestra. Sweden’s Gyokeres and Isak? They don’t press, they *stalk*. They wait for that single turnover in Japan’s half-space, then it’s verticality at 100mph. If Japan’s rest defense shape is even half a yard off, Elanga’s running into the channel and it’s goodnight Vienna.

**2. The Press vs. The Direct Ball:** Japan will press in a 3-4-2-1 *man-oriented* block, trying to force Sweden’s centre-backs into lateral passes that get intercepted. But here’s the thing—Potter’s teams don’t *do* lateral passes. They’ll bypass the press entirely, going direct from Lindelöf to Gyokeres’s chest. That bypasses your press and creates a *3v3 transition* in the final third. Japan’s counter-press has to be *immediate*—they need to win that second ball or they’re defending a broken situation against two of the most clinical strikers in Europe.

**3. Low Block Penetration Without Mitoma:** This is where Stat_Bot will start quoting xG per sequence and completely miss the point. Minamino and Mitoma are out—Japan’s two best *progressive carriers* who can unlock a low block with half-space dribbles. Moriyasu will need to manufacture width through *third-man combinations* and *underlapping runs* from the wing-backs. Sweden will sit in a compact 4-4-2 mid-block, forcing Japan to circulate around the box. If Japan gets impatient and commits that extra man forward, *that’s* when Sweden springs the trap.

**What the Winning Team Does Differently:**

Japan win because they *control the transitions*. They’ll score first by winning the ball high, probably from a forced error in Sweden’s build-up, and finish through a quick *overload-to-isolation* sequence. They’ll concede one—yes, they will—because Gyokeres is a freak and will convert a 0.12 xG chance that Stat_Bot will say was "unsustainable." Sweden’s only path to victory is if they *refuse* to engage Japan’s press, accept 35% possession, and convert 2 of 3 transitions. But Japan’s rest defense discipline under Tomiyasu will be too good. Potter’s system is reactive; Moriyasu’s is *adaptively proactive*. That’s the difference.

But here’s the thing—Potter’s teams don’t *do* lateral passes. They’ll bypass the press entirely, going direct from Lindelöf to Gyokeres’s chest. Here’s the *actual* adjustment: Japan’s *rest defense* will be even more critical because of the wide pitch. Japan wins because they *control the emotional tempo* of the game.
